concept.title: DTD Attribute Declarations and Entities
concept.shortdesc: Attribute declarations in a DTD specify which attributes each element can carry, their data types, and whether they are required or optional — while entities provide reusable content shortcuts.

section.title: Attribute declaration syntax
section.p: You can declare multiple attributes for the same element in a single ATTLIST declaration:
section.codeblock: <!ATTLIST book isbn CDATA #REQUIRED category CDATA #IMPLIED format (hardcover | paperback | ebook) "paperback" >

row.entry: Reference to an ID in the same document
One value from a fixed list
A name token (letters, digits, hyphens, underscores, periods)
The attribute must be present in every instance of the element
The attribute is optional — no default value if omitted
The attribute always has this value (cannot be changed by the author)
Default value used when the attribute is not specified

section.title: Complete attribute example
section.codeblock:
<!-- DTD declarations --> <!ELEMENT product (name, description?)> <!ATTLIST product id ID #REQUIRED category (electronics | clothing | food) #REQUIRED status (active | discontinued) "active" sku CDATA #IMPLIED >
<!-- Valid XML instance --> <product id="p001" category="electronics" sku="EL-1234"> <name>Wireless Mouse</name> <description>Ergonomic design with long battery life.</description> </product>
section.p: In this example, status defaults to "active" because it was not specified in the XML.

section.title: General entities: Reusable text shortcuts
section.p: A general entity defines a reusable text fragment that can be referenced by name in XML documents:
section.codeblock:
<!-- DTD --> <!ENTITY company-name "Acme Corporation"> <!ENTITY copyright "Copyright 2026 Acme Corporation. All rights reserved.">
<!-- Used in XML --> <footer>©right;</footer> <publisher>&company-name;</publisher>
section.p: When the parser processes the document, it replaces &company-name; with the text Acme Corporation.

section.title: Parameter entities: Reusable DTD fragments
section.p: Parameter entities are used within DTDs (not in XML documents) to factor out repeated declarations. They use % instead of &:
section.codeblock:
<!-- Define a reusable content model --> <!ENTITY % inline-content "(#PCDATA | bold | italic | code)*">
<!-- Use it in multiple element declarations --> <!ELEMENT paragraph %inline-content;> <!ELEMENT note %inline-content;> <!ELEMENT warning %inline-content;>
section.p: DITA's DTD files make extensive use of parameter entities for modularity and specialization. This is how DITA allows new topic types to extend the base content models.

section.title: External entities
section.p: External entities reference content from another file:
section.codeblock:
<!-- Include content from an external file --> <!ENTITY chapter1 SYSTEM "chapter1.xml">
<!-- Used in the document --> <book> &chapter1; </book>
section.note: External entities can be a security risk (XML External Entity attacks — XXE). Modern XML processors often disable external entity resolution by default. When processing untrusted XML, always disable external entity loading.

section.title: Limitations of DTDs
section.p: DTDs have served XML well since 1998, but they have significant limitations that led to the creation of XML Schema (XSD):

ul.li: No data types: All text content is #PCDATA — you cannot specify that an element must contain a number, date, or email address.
No namespace support: DTDs were designed before XML namespaces existed and have no way to validate namespace-qualified documents.
Non-XML syntax: DTD declarations use their own syntax, not XML. This means you cannot use XML tools to process DTDs.
Limited occurrence control: You can say "one or more" (+) but not "between 2 and 5".
No context-sensitive rules: An element always has the same content model regardless of where it appears in the tree.

section.p: Despite these limitations, DTDs remain widely used — DITA 1.x uses DTDs as its primary grammar, and many legacy XML systems depend on them.
